schools.summary: The start of the Peraean ministry; Christ crosses the Jordan toward Jerusalem and the cross, healing to authenticate his teaching.
Allegory: Christ leaving Father and synagogue to cleave to the Church (Ephesians 5:32); the move keeps the Gentiles in view.
Chrysostom: he frequents Judaea now because the passion is near, mingling miracle with teaching.
Origen: healing beyond Jordan figures baptism; few 'arise and follow' to the twelve thrones.
Henry: Christ's final farewell to Galilee, leaving only when his testimony there was finished.
Crowds gather from all the surrounding regions; Christ teaches and heals, the miracles sealing his doctrine.
Clarke notes the mixed motives of the crowd — instruction, healing, curiosity, and entrapment.
The Shammai/Hillel dispute over Deuteronomy 24:1; a snare sharpened by Herodian politics in Peraea (Herod Antipas, Josephus).
Henry: Christ proves the indissoluble union by creation, the leaving of parents, and one-flesh — what God joined, let none sunder.
Calvin: the malicious question yields a fixed law of indissoluble marriage grounded in the order of creation.
Origen: to tempters Christ replies with doctrine, not evasion.
Christ answers from creation and Moses, not Shammai or Hillel; the original design fixes marriage as permanent, not subject to caprice.
Calvin: the 'two' of creation proves one husband with one wife, condemning polygamy as well as divorce.
Wesley: one man and one woman condemns polygamy; one flesh condemns divorce.
Adam's words are a divine oracle (Holy Spirit); 'cleave'/'one flesh' marks a union closer than any other, sealed in the offspring.
Calvin: argues from the lesser (parent-child) to the greater (marriage); honours God's order, husband as head not tyrant, condemns polygamy.
Origen: 'one flesh' figures Christ and the Church (Ephesians 5:31-32); Christ left the Father for his bride.
'What God yoked' — marriage rests on the natural fact of union, not law or ceremony; systems easing divorce treat the permanent as temporary.
Calvin: Christ restrains husbands' caprice and forbids magistrates to confirm unlawful divorce; the magistrate abuses his power who permits it.
Calvin: Moses permitted (not commanded) divorce for hardness of heart, to protect women — a punishment on husbands, distinct from spiritual law.
The laxer school appeals to the letter (Deuteronomy 24:1); men wrest Scripture to justify lust.
Origen: the law must not be read by the letter alone — 'from the beginning it was not so.'
'Suffered,' not 'commanded': a concession choosing the lesser of two evils; the creation ideal stands unrepealed (perfect tense).
Origen: some laws accommodate human weakness, as Paul's concessions do (1 Corinthians 7); the higher law is 'from the beginning.'

outliers.note: Reads 'hardness of heart' as obduration against God (idolatry), carrying a note of punishment.

schools.summary: Fornication alone dissolves the one-flesh bond; remarriage while the first lives is forbidden — yet reconciliation after repentance is open (Augustine).
Christ proclaims his own law; only adultery permits divorce (a mensa et thoro); Robertson defends the genuineness of the exception.
Calvin: the right is mutual to husband and wife, and the innocent party is freed to remarry.

debates.question: May the innocent party remarry after divorcing an adulterous spouse?

sides.position: No — remarriage while the former spouse lives is adultery
Yes — Christ permits the innocent party to remarry

schools.summary: The disciples voice the popular Hillel view; Ellicott likens it to Milton's tract; Christ's doctrine was too high even for the Twelve.
Continence is a gift sought from God by grace; the prize is for those who will struggle, not shun the contest.
Calvin: their selfish recoil rejects a gift of God; he rebukes Jerome's calumnies against marriage, a medicine for fallen nature.
'This saying' = the disciples' conclusion; celibacy suits few (1 Corinthians 7); for the rest marriage is the safer state.
Calvin: the choice is not ours — continence is a special gift not given to all; self-imposed vows of celibacy reprove God's appointment.
Clarke/Wesley: neither state is condemned; the gift comes by nature, violence, or grace.
The third eunuch is spiritual — a bridling of thoughts, not bodily mutilation (which is cursed); the gift is for kingdom service.
The Essenes as analogue; Origen's literal self-mutilation lamented; celibacy must be for the kingdom, not imposed as a rule (Rome's error).
Calvin: celibacy is no virtue in itself but freedom for God's service; he condemns the binding vow of perpetual virginity.

outliers.note: Recalls Origen's literal self-mutilation as a misreading of the verse.

schools.summary: Christ welcomes infancy ('of such,' by disposition not years); Hilary reads the children as a type of the believing Gentiles.
The disciples' rough indifference rebuked; Lightfoot reads believers' infants as disciples — a warrant for infant baptism.
Henry: children of believers belong to the visible church — 'the promise is to you, and to your children.'
Calvin: a shield against the Anabaptists — Christ receives those too young to know their need, and gives them purity.
Christ's indignant tenderness; the kingdom belongs to such 'as by inheritance'; hope (Barnes) that infants are saved.
Calvin: grace and baptism extend to infants whom God adopts; yet 'of such' also means the childlike, childlike in malice not understanding.
Clarke: the verse ruins 'non-elect infants' damnation'; Christ sanctified childhood by passing through it.
Christ laid hands and truly blessed the children, then resumed toward Jericho; Ellicott reads the act as the warrant for infant baptism.
Jerome reads him as proud and tempting; Remigius/Raban draw the active vs contemplative life and the call to sell all.
Chrysostom: not a hypocrite but a captive of wealth — 'Jesus loved him.'
Henry: a grave, good question; Christ leads him to own him as good God and to enter life by obedience.
A blameless but unsatisfied ruler depending on works; Ellicott conjectures he may be Lazarus.
Calvin: he dreams of merit; Christ sends him to the law as the way of righteousness to expose his self-trust.

debates.question: Did the young ruler come sincerely or to tempt Christ?

outliers.note: Conjectures the young ruler may be Lazarus of Bethany.

schools.summary: A reproof of flattery, not a disclaimer of deity; a Socratic method (Ellicott); the variant 'concerning the good' still refutes anti-Trinitarian misuse.
Calvin: Christ maintains the authority of his doctrine; the law is true righteousness but impossible to us, driving us to faith (Romans 10:5-6).
Wesley: a plain, serious answer, not irony — believe, and thence love and obey.
The second table is cited as the test of love to God (Lightfoot); the reordering leads from negative prohibitions to the 'broad' positive law.
Calvin: the second table best scrutinizes the heart (hypocrites feign the first); the fifth commandment belongs to it, against Josephus.
Clarke: the commandments are not kept to purchase life; Christ must save from sin.
The fifth commandment cited last ('no first or last in the law'); 'love thy neighbour as thyself' (Leviticus 19:18) caps the second table.
Clarke: rightly understood self-love drives the soul to God, who alone constitutes its happiness; sin against God wrongs the soul.
Cyril: honour earthly parents under the heavenly Father, obeying except where they oppose godliness.
A sincere but shallow claim, kept as to the letter not the spirit; an uneasy conscience and 'psychological paradox' (Robertson) that opens the way for Christ.
A test of the man's idol, not a universal rule; 'perfect' = complete; the spirit binds all under wealth's power, the letter does not (Ellicott).
Clarke/Wesley: Christ probes the bosom sin of love of the world — a particular direction, never a general rule.
Cyril: wealth is not the devil's; rightly used, money becomes a door of the kingdom.
His wealth was an idol; he had not truly kept the law; one sin is enough to keep one from Christ (Robertson).
Clarke: the agony of suspense between love of the world and love of the soul; idolatry of goods brings misery.

outliers.note: Sees the sorrow as hopeful conflict, and conjectures the later Lazarus discipline completed the work.

schools.summary: The danger is trust in riches, not riches; the camel/needle (and 'needle's eye' gate) figure the rich entering only when the load of sin is laid aside.
Chrysostom: not riches but enslavement to them; 'hard' rises to 'impossible,' then relieved by 'with God all things are possible.'
Henry: more duties and more besetting sins make the rich man's way hard; grace alone saves him from his worldliness, not in it.
Calvin: riches are not evil by nature, but few escape intoxication by them; the warning profits rich and poor alike.
A proverb of impossibility (cf. the Talmud's elephant, the Koran's borrowing); attempts to soften it as 'cable' or a 'needle's eye' gate are needless.
Calvin leans to 'cable,' but the bold Eastern image of the camel needs no softening — the thing is humanly impossible.
Wesley: 'Rich man, tremble — feel this impossibility, else thou art lost forever.'
Blank amazement; the disciples counted on outward riches; Mark's softening clause is likely a copyist's gloss reflecting their feeling.
Calvin: unlike the departing ruler, the trembling disciples do not break off but seek to conquer despair — wholesome trembling that arouses.
Clarke defines the convicting sense of 'rich man'; Wesley: a beggar is saved sooner than one rich.
The searching look of wonder and tenderness; salvation impossible to man is the work of God's grace — wealth may even become a help.
Calvin: Christ heightens, not softens, the difficulty to drive self-distrust and prayer; the verse refutes the anathema against owning the law impossible to keep.
Clarke/Wesley: God alone removes the love of the world; the look is sweet, the saying strengthened not retracted.
Chrysostom: Peter's 'all' was reeds and a net, but he asks for the poor's sake — none is shut out by poverty.
The 'following' (added to forsaking) is peculiar to believers, unlike pagan philosophers who forsook wealth; Peter the athlete asks his prize.
A poor all, yet truly their all; but the question betrays a self-interested, letter-keeping spirit expecting compensation.
Calvin: the inquiry is fair, but they err in demanding the triumph before finishing the warfare.

'Regeneration' read variously — the resurrection/judgment (Barnes), the gospel renewal (Lightfoot), the restitution of all things (Ellicott); thrones = honour, conditioned on faithfulness (Judas's vacant).
Hilary: regeneration = baptism; Augustine: 'twelve' = the fullness of all judges (Paul too); the age of the Church (Pseudo-Chrysostom).
Calvin: 'regeneration' began at Christ's first coming, but the thrones belong to the last day when the apostles share his glory.

sides.position: The final resurrection / renewal of the cosmos
The gospel age / renewal of the Church

schools.summary: The promise widens to all believers; the hundredfold is spiritual (multiplied joys and relationships), given 'with persecutions,' crowned by eternal life.
Calvin: God's gladdening grace makes the believer's small portion sweeter than the wicked's abundance, answering the objection from experience.
Clarke/Wesley: fullness of grace here and glory hereafter, for every believer in every age, not the apostles only.
A reversal of human judgments aimed at the disciples' self-complacence, introducing the parable of the labourers (Matthew 20:16).
Clarke: the redemptive-historical reading — first Jews rejecting the gospel become last, last Gentiles become first.
Henry: glory goes by grace, not seniority — God crosses his hands, receiving believing Gentiles over unbelieving Jews.
